sections.text: When you type a website name like example.com into your browser, how does your computer know where to find it? The answer is a system called DNS, which works like a phone book for the internet.

sections.title: What is DNS?
sections.text: DNS stands for Domain Name System. Every computer on the internet has an address made of numbers, called an IP address. Humans are not good at remembering long strings of numbers, so DNS lets us use easy names instead, like google.com, and it quietly looks up the matching number for us.

sections.title: Why Do We Need It?
sections.text: Without DNS, you would have to remember a number like 142.250.190.78 just to visit a website, instead of typing a simple name. DNS translates the easy name you type into the number that computers actually use to find each other on the internet.

sections.title: How DNS Looks Up a Website
items: You type a website name into your browser, like example.com.
Your browser asks a DNS server: what is the IP address for this name?
The DNS server looks it up and sends back the matching IP address.
Your browser now knows exactly which computer on the internet to talk to.
Your browser connects to that IP address and asks the server for the website.

sections.title: Think of it like a Phone Book
sections.text: Imagine you want to call your friend but you only remember their name, not their phone number. You look up their name in a phone book, and it tells you the number to dial. DNS does the exact same thing, but instead of names and phone numbers, it matches website names to IP addresses.
DNS lookups usually happen so fast, often in a tiny fraction of a second, that you never even notice them happening before your page loads.

terms.definition: Domain Name System, the system that turns website names into IP addresses.
The easy to remember name of a website, like example.com.
A number that identifies a specific computer on the internet.
A computer that keeps a list of domain names and their matching IP addresses.
The process of asking a DNS server for the IP address that matches a domain name.

options: It turns easy website names into IP addresses
It draws pictures on the screen
questions.explanation: DNS, or Domain Name System, takes the easy name you type, like example.com, and finds the matching IP address so your browser knows where to connect.

options: A number that identifies a specific computer on the internet
questions.explanation: An IP address is a number that identifies a specific computer on the internet, similar to how a phone number identifies a specific phone.

questions.text: In the phone book analogy, what does DNS act like?
options: A phone book that matches names to numbers
questions.explanation: Just like a phone book matches a name to a phone number, DNS matches a domain name to an IP address.
